title: Houthis Strike Saudi Tankers as Red Sea Shuts Down, Lebanon Deploys Troops to Pilot Zone Under Fire, US Accuses China of AI Theft
subhead: Yemen's Houthis attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea — the first strikes since declaring a maritime embargo — as Lebanon's army entered a US-brokered pilot zone only to draw Israeli warning fire, and Washington formally accused Beijing's Moonshot AI of stealing US model capabilities.

sections.heading: Houthis Strike Saudi Tankers as Both Gulf Chokepoints Close
sections.body:
Yemen's Houthi movement claimed attacks on two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea on Wednesday — the first confirmed strikes since the group declared a 'maritime embargo' against Saudi Arabia on Monday. Houthi military spokesperson Yahya Saree said the group targeted the tankers Encelia and Layla with drones and missiles for violating its naval blockade, vowing to continue 'enforcing the siege-for-a-siege equation.' The UK Maritime Trade Operation confirmed an 'unknown projectile' struck a Saudi tanker roughly 130km from Al Shuqaiq, causing a fire but no casualties. British maritime risk group Vanguard also reported the Encelia had been hit, according to Reuters.

The attacks effectively close the last alternative route for Saudi crude. Saudi Arabia had diverted more than 70% of its exports through its east-west pipeline to the Red Sea port of Yanbu since the Iran war shut down the Strait of Hormuz — roughly four million barrels per day, up from 973,000 a year earlier, according to Kpler and Signal Ocean data. At least seven oil tankers have already made U-turns near Bab al-Mandab, and Saree claimed the Houthis have 'forced approximately ten ships to retreat.' The EU's naval force Aspides has recommended that vessels linked to Israeli, US, or Saudi interests avoid the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden entirely.

Meanwhile, Iran's Revolutionary Guard declared the Strait of Hormuz 'completely closed' after an explosion in the channel, with Iranian state media reporting one tanker caught fire and two others turned back after attempting a mined route. Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi warned of an 'eye for an eye' response to attacks. Oil prices rose to $94 per barrel. Kpler analyst Naveen Das said the only remaining option for Saudi crude to Asia would be the Mediterranean route around Africa's southern tip, adding weeks and significant cost: 'We could weather it for two or three weeks, even a month, but even in that time, we will see higher freight rates, higher energy prices, and that bleeds into higher consumer costs.'
sections.whyItMatters: With both Hormuz and Bab al-Mandab effectively blocked, the world's top oil exporter has no safe maritime route to Asian markets. The economic ripple will reach consumers within weeks, not months.

sections.heading: Lebanon's Army Enters Pilot Zone, Draws Israeli Warning Fire
sections.body:
Lebanon's army began deploying troops to a 'pilot zone' in the country's south on Tuesday under a US-brokered agreement that envisions Israeli withdrawal from a small occupied area and the disarmament of Hezbollah. Engineering units entered the village of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh in Nabatieh district, with operations also underway in the nearby villages of Froun and Srifa, the army command announced.

Almost immediately, the deployment ran into trouble. The Lebanese army said Israeli forces 'opened fire in the vicinity of these units' and warned that the attack 'hinders the implementation of deployment steps in the pilot zones.' The Israeli military said its soldiers fired 'warning shots into the air' after Lebanese troops and an engineering vehicle 'crossed approximately 150m into the security zone and breached the barriers' in neighbouring Zawtar al-Sharqiyeh. The troops withdrew and no injuries were reported. Lebanon's National News Agency also reported Israeli controlled demolitions near Beit Yahoun, Kounine, and Majdal Zoun.

The deployment coincided with a White House meeting between President Trump and Lebanese President Joseph Aoun — the first Lebanese head of state to visit Washington in nearly 17 years. Aoun said he asked Trump for 'political support and supporting the LAF,' while Trump vowed the US would help Lebanon, which had been 'hit hard' for decades. The US State Department called the pilot zone a 'milestone' resulting from Rome discussions between Israeli and Lebanese representatives. Hezbollah has rejected the framework entirely, saying linking Israeli withdrawal to disarmament crossed 'all red lines.' Israel says it will not fully withdraw until Hezbollah is disarmed.
sections.whyItMatters: The pilot zone is the first tangible step toward ending Israel's occupation of southern Lebanon, but Israeli warning fire during the very first deployment shows how fragile the arrangement is. With Hezbollah rejecting the deal outright and Israel insisting on full disarmament before withdrawal, the pilot zone could collapse before it scales.

sections.heading: US Accuses China's Moonshot AI of Stealing American Model Capabilities
sections.body:
White House science and technology adviser Michael Kratsios accused Beijing-based Moonshot AI of a 'large scale' effort to steal capabilities from top US AI models through distillation — extracting outputs from a stronger model to train a weaker one. Kratsios said Moonshot AI 'distilled' capabilities from Anthropic's models for the development of Kimi K3, a Chinese AI model that gained global attention after its release last week, with many analysts believing it has narrowed the gap between Western and Chinese AI.

The accusation came a day after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the US would examine whether Chinese AI models had stolen capabilities from American rivals, and on Wednesday Bessent added that sanctions 'will be on the table' when Chinese companies 'cross the line' into IP theft through 'industrial-scale distillation attacks.' Kratsios also said Moonshot was likely using servers powered by Nvidia's GB300 Grace Blackwell platform — chips whose export to China Washington restricted in 2022 over military concerns. Moonshot AI will release Kimi K3 as open-source on 27 July, making it the first model of its scale freely downloadable by the public.

Anthropic urged Congress to penalise companies behind the alleged attacks and tighten controls on US technology. The Chinese government has not yet responded publicly but has previously said its AI development is 'the result of its own dedication and effort as well as international cooperation.' The accusations come ahead of an expected Trump-Xi meeting in September.
sections.whyItMatters: The US-China AI rivalry is escalating from export controls to formal IP theft accusations. If Washington sanctions Moonshot AI, it would open a new front in the technology cold war — and Kimi K3's open-source release on 27 July could make enforcement near-impossible once the weights are public.

sections.heading: Trump Threatens Iranian Civilian Infrastructure as War Costs Mount
sections.body:
President Trump warned that the US would destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant — potentially near Tehran — every time Iranian forces attack a ship in the Strait of Hormuz, 'whether it be by missile, rocket, drone, or any other device or weapon.' The statement, posted on Truth Social, marks an explicit escalation: the US is now tying civilian infrastructure targets to specific Iranian actions at sea. Separately, he said the next target could be an underground complex known as Pickaxe Mountain near Natanz, where Iran is suspected of building an undeclared enrichment facility.

US strikes on Iran continued for a 12th consecutive night, with explosions reported near Tehran, Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, and Bushehr — home to Iran's only operational nuclear power plant. Iranian state media reported two people killed near the Iraq border. Iran retaliated with drone and missile launches targeting US assets in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan, with all three countries reporting intercepts. A fourth US service member killed in the region was identified — Sgt Angel Rampersad, 28 — bringing total US fatalities to 18. Trump attended a dignified transfer ceremony at Dover Air Force Base.

Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee that the war has cost $37.5 billion so far — up nearly $8 billion since the last estimate in May — and requested an additional $87 billion, with $67 billion destined for Middle East operations. The House passed a $95 billion budget package largely funding the Iran war, but it faces an uphill battle in the Senate. The hearing was disrupted by anti-war protesters and ended in a shouting match between Hegseth and Democratic Senator Gary Peters, who called the war 'another forever war.' Democratic Senator Kirsten Gillibrand accused the administration of seeking 'unlimited money for bombs' while American citizens 'cannot afford to feed their families.' Multiple Democratic sources told CBS the true cost of the war is likely much higher than $37.5 billion, as the figure excludes construction for damaged American bases.
sections.whyItMatters: Both sides are now explicitly threatening civilian infrastructure. Trump's one-attack-one-strike framing creates an automatic escalation mechanism, and the $37.5 billion cost figure — likely an underestimate — will shape the upcoming Senate vote on war funding.

sections.heading: Gaza Death Toll Rises as UN Warns of Possible War Crimes
sections.body:
At least 12 Palestinians, including six members of one family, were killed in three separate Israeli strikes in the Gaza Strip, according to local medics. The al-Masri family — father Firas, mother Salsabeel, and their four children Faryal, Salma, Amira, and Naim — were killed when their apartment was struck in the western al-Sabra neighbourhood of Gaza City. The Israeli military said the target was a Hamas operative and that it was still investigating the results. Four more were killed in a strike on a home in al-Samar and two in a drone strike on a vehicle in al-Zahra.

The UN's human rights office condemned the recent intensification of Israeli military attacks in Gaza. Between 13 and 20 July, at least 57 Palestinians — including six children and eight women — were killed in strikes on residential buildings, a tent camp for displaced families, and other crowded areas, spokesman Thameen Al-Kheetan said. Thirty-four of those were killed 'far away' from the Israeli-controlled Yellow Line, he added. 'Nine months after the announcement of a ceasefire, still, nowhere is safe for Palestinians in Gaza,' Al-Kheetan said, warning that the killing of civilians 'raises concerns of continuing violations of international humanitarian law, war crimes and other possible atrocity crimes.' According to Gaza's Hamas-run health ministry, at least 1,168 Palestinians have been killed in Israeli attacks since the ceasefire took effect last October, while four Israeli soldiers have been killed in Palestinian attacks in Gaza over the same period.
sections.whyItMatters: The UN's explicit reference to possible war crimes marks a significant rhetorical escalation, and the rising post-ceasefire death toll — 1,168 Palestinians killed since October — underscores that the Gaza ceasefire exists in name only.

sections.heading: Canada's Dairy Sector in Trump's Crosshairs as 50% Tariffs Loom
sections.body:
Trump has singled out Canada's dairy supply management system as one of three main irritants justifying a 50% tariff on $20 billion worth of Canadian goods set to take effect in August. The system, in place since the early 1970s, sets production quotas, fixed pricing, and import quotas on dairy, eggs, and poultry — with tariffs of 200% to nearly 300% on imports exceeding quota limits. US producers currently have tariff-free access to only 3.5% of Canada's dairy market, even as Canada bought $1.3 billion worth of US dairy products in 2025, according to USDA data.

Canadian politicians have so far signalled that dairy is non-negotiable. Quebec Premier Christine Frechette called supply management 'non-negotiable,' while Trade Minister Dominic LeBlanc said it is 'a cornerstone of Canada's economy and our rural communities.' David Clement of the Consumer Choice Center called dairy 'the most powerful political lobby in the country that stretches across all of the major political parties.' Around 77% of Canadians support keeping the system in place, polling shows, and dairy farmers have previously driven tractors and cattle onto Parliament Hill to protest trade concessions.

A White House order released Monday argues that Canada's free trade agreement with the EU makes it easier for European producers to sell cheese in Canada than for US producers, calling it 'discrimination.' Even some Canadian economists have called for reform, noting that Canadians paid an average of C$3.19 per litre of milk in May versus C$1.95 in the US. The Biden administration also twice challenged Canada's dairy quota practices under USMCA, and the UK walked away from trade talks in 2024 over the same issue.
sections.whyItMatters: Canada's dairy system has survived every previous challenge, but a 50% tariff on $20 billion of Canadian goods is economic pressure on a scale Ottawa has never faced. The collision between a politically untouchable domestic policy and an unprecedented trade weapon is now unavoidable.

bottomLine: Both Gulf chokepoints are now effectively closed — Hormuz by Iran, Bab al-Mandab by the Houthis — leaving Saudi Arabia without a safe maritime route for its crude and pushing oil toward $100. Lebanon's pilot zone deployment drew Israeli warning fire within hours, exposing the fragility of the only diplomatic framework for ending the occupation of the south. Washington formally accused China's Moonshot AI of stealing US model capabilities, opening a new front in the technology rivalry just days before Kimi K3 goes open-source. And Trump's threat to destroy Iranian civilian infrastructure for every attack on shipping creates an automatic escalation mechanism that makes de-escalation harder with each passing day.
